quality.rationale: 基于静态引力场度规与能量动量守恒律进行多步积分推导，清晰论证重量仅取决于质能密度，物理动机明确且解答完整。

origin.question: [文件 1: 1_s14_14.6.md]Problem 14.6. Two identical containers in a static uniform gravitational field contain different substances. The containers and contents have identical time-independent densities of mass-energy, but different (and possibly anisotropic) stresses. Do the containers weigh the same if weighed on the same scale?## SolutionSolution 14.6. A static uniform gravitational field has a line element$$\mathrm {d s}^{2} = \mathrm {g}_{\mathrm {t t}} \mathrm {d t}^{2} + \mathrm {g}_{\mathrm {z z}} \mathrm {d z}^{2} + \mathrm {d x}^{2} + \mathrm {d y}^{2} \tag {1}$$where $\mathbf{g}_{tt}$ and $\mathbf{g}_{zz}$ depend only on $z$ . The nonzero Christoffel symbols are easily computed (Problem 7.6). The weight as measured by a scale at $z = 0$ is $\mathbf{W}(0)$ , where$$W (z) = \int T^{\widehat {z} \widehat {z}} d x d y = \int g_{z z} T^{z z} d x d y. \tag {2}$$We wish to show that $W(0)$ depends only on $T^{\widehat{tt}} = g_{tt}T^{tt}$ and on no other $T^{\widehat{a}\widehat{\beta}}$ . We have$$0 = T^{\alpha \beta}; \beta = | g |^{- \frac {1}{2}} \left(| g |^{\frac {1}{2}} T^{\alpha \beta}\right), \beta + \Gamma^{\alpha}_{\beta \gamma} T^{\beta \gamma}. \tag {3}$$Take $\alpha = t$ and integrate over $x, y$ , using the fact that $g_{\alpha \beta}$ is independent of $x, y$ and $t$ :$$\begin{array}{l} 0 = \int T^{t t}_{, t} d x d y + \int T^{t x}_{, x} d x d y + \int T^{t y}_{, y} d x d y + | g |^{- \frac {1}{2}} (| g |^{\frac {1}{2}} \int T^{t z} d x d y)_{, z} \\ + 2 \Gamma^{t}_{t z} \int T^{t z} d x d y. \tag {4} \\ \end{array}$$The $1^{\text{st}}$ term vanishes because $\mathbf{T}^{\text{tt}}$ is time-independent. The $2^{\text{nd}}$ and $3^{\text{rd}}$ terms give terms proportional to $\mathbf{T}^{\text{tx}}$ and $\mathbf{T}^{\text{ty}}$ on the boundary of the integration region. These vanish for a boundary outside the container.Equation (4) can be put in the form$$0 = | g |^{- \frac {1}{2}} g_{t t}^{- 1} \left(| g |^{\frac {1}{2}} g_{t t} \int T^{t z} d x d y\right)_{, z}. \tag {5}$$Thus $|g|^{\frac{1}{2}} g_{tt} \int T^{tz} \, dx \, dy$ is independent of $z$ . Since this term vanishes above the top of the container, it vanishes everywhere.Now put $a = z$ in Equation (3) and integrate over $x$ and $y$ :$$\begin{array}{l} 0 = \int T^{z t},_{t} d x d y + \int T^{z x},_{x} d x d y + \int T^{z y},_{y} d x d y + | g |^{- \frac {1}{2}} (| g |^{\frac {1}{2}} \int T^{z z} d x d y),_{z} \\ + \Gamma^{z}_{t t} \int \mathrm {T}^{t t} d x d y + \Gamma^{z}_{z z} \int \mathrm {T}^{z z} d x d y. \tag {6} \\ \end{array}$$The $1^{\text{st}}$ term vanishes as above, while the $2^{\text{nd}}$ and $3^{\text{rd}}$ give boundary contributions which vanish. Using Equation (2), we find$$\left(\left| g_{t t}\right|^{\frac {1}{2}} W\right)_{, z} = \left| g_{t t}\right|^{\frac {1}{2}}_{, z} \int \mathrm {T}^{\widehat {t t}} d x d y. \tag {7}$$Integrate Equation (7) from $z = 0$ to the top of the container, and use the fact that $W(top) = 0$ to get$$W (0) = \left| g_{t t}\right|_{z = 0}^{- \frac {1}{2}} \int_{0}^{\text {top}} d z \int d x d y \left| g_{t t}\right|^{\frac {1}{2}},_{z} T^{\hat {t} \hat {t}}, \tag {8}$$which depends only on the component $\mathbf{T}^{\widehat{\mathbf{t}}\mathbf{t}}$ . (Solution due to W. Unruh.)
origin.answer: Yes, the containers weigh the same.
origin.solution: Solution 14.6. A static uniform gravitational field has a line element$$ds^{2} = g_{tt} dt^{2} + g_{zz} dz^{2} + dx^{2} + dy^{2} \tag {1}$$where $\mathbf{g}_{tt}$ and $\mathbf{g}_{zz}$ depend only on $z$ . The nonzero Christoffel symbols are easily computed (Problem 7.6). The weight as measured by a scale at $z = 0$ is $\mathbf{W}(0)$ , where$$W (z) = \int T^{\widehat {z} \widehat {z}} d x d y = \int g_{z z} T^{z z} d x d y. \tag {2}$$We wish to show that $W(0)$ depends only on $T^{\widehat{tt}} = g_{tt}T^{tt}$ and on no other $T^{\widehat{a}\widehat{\beta}}$ . We have$$0 = T^{\alpha \beta}; \beta = | g |^{- \frac {1}{2}} \left(| g |^{\frac {1}{2}} T^{\alpha \beta}\right), \beta + \Gamma^{\alpha}_{\beta \gamma} T^{\beta \gamma}. \tag {3}$$Take $\alpha = t$ and integrate over $x, y$ , using the fact that $g_{\alpha \beta}$ is independent of $x, y$ and $t$ :$$\begin{array}{l} 0 = \int T^{t t}_{, t} d x d y + \int T^{t x}_{, x} d x d y + \int T^{t y}_{, y} d x d y + | g |^{- \frac {1}{2}} (| g |^{\frac {1}{2}} \int T^{t z} d x d y)_{, z} \\ + 2 \Gamma^{t}_{t z} \int T^{t z} d x d y. \tag {4} \\ \end{array}$$The $1^{\text{st}}$ term vanishes because $\mathbf{T}^{\text{tt}}$ is time-independent. The $2^{\text{nd}}$ and $3^{\text{rd}}$ terms give terms proportional to $\mathbf{T}^{\text{tx}}$ and $\mathbf{T}^{\text{ty}}$ on the boundary of the integration region. These vanish for a boundary outside the container.Equation (4) can be put in the form$$0 = | g |^{- \frac {1}{2}} g_{tt}^{- 1} \left(| g |^{\frac {1}{2}} g_{tt} \int T^{tz} dx dy\right)_{, z}. \tag {5}$$Thus $|g|^{\frac{1}{2}} g_{tt} \int T^{tz} \, dx \, dy$ is independent of $z$ . Since this term vanishes above the top of the container, it vanishes everywhere.Now put $a = z$ in Equation (3) and integrate over $x$ and $y$ :$$\begin{array}{l} 0 = \int T^{zt},_{t} d x d y + \int T^{zx},_{x} d x d y + \int T^{zy},_{y} d x d y + | g |^{- \frac {1}{2}} (| g |^{\frac {1}{2}} \int T^{zz} d x d y),_{z} \\ + \Gamma^{z}_{tt} \int T^{tt} dx dy + \Gamma^{z}_{zz} \int T^{zz} dx dy. \tag {6} \\ \end{array}$$The $1^{\text{st}}$ term vanishes as above, while the $2^{\text{nd}}$ and $3^{\text{rd}}$ give boundary contributions which vanish. Using Equation (2), we find$$\left(\left| g_{tt}\right|^{\frac {1}{2}} W\right)_{, z} = \left| g_{tt}\right|^{\frac {1}{2}}_{, z} \int T^{\widehat {tt}} dx dy. \tag {7}$$Integrate Equation (7) from $z = 0$ to the top of the container, and use the fact that $W(top) = 0$ to get$$W (0) = \left| g_{tt}\right|_{z = 0}^{- \frac {1}{2}} \int_{0}^{\text {top}} dz \int dx dy \left| g_{tt}\right|^{\frac {1}{2}},_{z} T^{\hat {t} \hat {t}}, \tag {8}$$which depends only on the component $\mathbf{T}^{\widehat{\mathbf{t}}\mathbf{t}}$ . (Solution due to W. Unruh.)
